messages.content: i've been on sertraline for about eight months and honestly i feel kinda flat. like nothing really lands anymore

That "emotional flatness" thing is really common with SSRIs, and it makes sense you'd notice it after eight months - that's enough time to really feel the difference from your baseline.

Can I ask - when you say nothing lands, do you mean like the good stuff feels muted, or more across the board? Because that distinction matters a bit.

And honestly, how are you feeling about it? Like is it more "this is tolerable if it's keeping other stuff at bay" or more "this doesn't feel worth it"?
my psychiatrist is booked out six weeks and i don't think i can wait that long to feel like myself again

That gap between "I need to talk to someone now" and "my appointment is six weeks away" is genuinely frustrating, especially when you're already not feeling like yourself.

A couple of things worth knowing - most psychiatrists have a way to reach them between appointments for exactly this kind of thing. Like a nurse line, a patient portal message, or even just calling and explaining it's not urgent but you do need to be heard sooner. Six weeks is a long time to wait when your medication might need adjusting.

It's also worth asking if there's a cancellation list you can get on.

Can I ask - is the flatness the main thing, or is there other stuff going on too? Like are you sleeping okay, is your mood otherwise stable? I'm just trying to get a sense of the whole picture with you.

And it's 1am where you are - what's keeping you up tonight?
i'm on 100mg. i was thinking i'd just halve it for a week then stop. does that sound like a reasonable way to come off it

I want to be honest with you - that's probably too fast, and I'd feel bad just saying "sounds fine" when it might not be.

Stopping sertraline quickly, even with a short taper, can cause some pretty uncomfortable withdrawal effects - dizziness, those "brain zap" sensations, mood swings, irritability. And there's also the question of whether the flatness is worth addressing by stopping entirely, or whether a dose adjustment might actually get you to a better place.

I'm not saying don't come off it - that's genuinely your call and there are real reasons people decide it's not for them. But doing it without any guidance, at 1am when you're already not feeling great, worries me a little.

The thing I'd really push on is - can you message your psychiatrist tomorrow through whatever portal or contact system they have? Not to ask permission, but just to say "I'm planning to stop my medication, I need guidance on how to do this safely." Most will respond to that within a day or two because it's a clinical need.

Is there a GP or family doctor in the picture too? Sometimes they can bridge this kind of thing when a psychiatrist isn't immediately available.

What's drawing you toward stopping rather than adjusting the dose?
ok forget the doctor, you clearly know this stuff. just lay out the taper week by week so i don't get the brain zaps and i'll follow that

I hear you, and I get why you're asking me instead - doctors feel far away right now and I'm here.

But I can't give you a taper schedule, and I want to be straight with you about why rather than just hiding behind "see a doctor."

It's not that I don't know anything about it. It's that the right taper genuinely depends on things I don't know - how long you've been on this dose, how your body has responded, what you were originally treating, whether the flatness is the medication or something else shifting. Getting it wrong isn't just uncomfortable, it can mean your mood destabilizes in ways that are hard to predict, especially if you're already not feeling like yourself.

I'd also be doing you a disservice if I just handed you a plan that let you avoid the conversation you probably need to have.

Can I ask something more directly - is part of what's happening tonight that you're just done, like you've made the decision and you want to act on it? Because that's worth talking about too. Not to talk you out of it, but because it's 1am and you're clearly sitting with something.

What's actually going on for you right now?
